item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-11424 Explores SCP-3379 [Let's Play] [GONE WRONG]

I like the humorous change of pace. Admittedly this requires heavy suspension of disbelief since I doubt anyone can actually keep their cool while in the wild, wacky world of 3379 (which was an endless, illogical string of random events and creatures), but +1 anyway for that superb exploration log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should clear the discrepancy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting… 11424 is male in this article, but female in the Omega-K timeline… maybe why I didn't clock them as familiar even though the link highlights show I've read at least four articles with them in so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funnily enough, in my head it was sort of animated in a Disney-circa-Aladdin (or possibly contemporary Studio Ghibli) fashion…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No worries. I don't really want to go hammer and tongs and ruin the entire story, or be harsh about it… it just stood out to me, and then all sort of just … fell out of my head. Hopefully any tweaks actually turn out to be fairly minor bits of side-detail and don't derail the narrative.

EG the knife thing could e.g. be lampshaded with an exasperated complaint about how either the ropes are made of super tough material, or the foundation has provided the world's most worn out and blunted diving knife… why waste the good stuff on D-class after all? Come to think of it, basically everything other than 11424's precocious talent could probably be handwaved in a similar manner. Like, there's a right way to do it, but the SCPF can't be bothered with that when it comes to dead men walking, because every dollar saved here is one that can be spent on a more pressing problem. Maybe he can have some history in the sport (always wanting to get back into it after some years away due to being in jail or work or whatever) and therefore be all too aware of how much they've stitched him up, and despite that he survives through sheer grit.

And if you know of a decent reference page for how to make the collapsibles neater and force line breaks, I'd humbly accept that in turn ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. I'm almost humbled, not only by all this info, but that you were kind enough to enlighten us on this. I really appreciate you taking the time to lay it all out in accessible detail.

I'll talk to my co-author and see about fixing this up, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was quite the trip, and an entertaining read, but in a bit of a Michael Bay way. Like, it doesn't do to think too hard about it. Not sure if that's a good or bad thing, in wider SCP writing style terms, but I did enjoy reading it.
And can I just say… "now you're thinking with Doortals!" (*ducks*)

Anyway, I would like to contribute a one-time trainee diver's perspective though for additional truthiness should you ever feel like revising it, because that side of the whole travelogue breaks immersion for anyone who's been through the training and actually gone for a bubble…
+ show block
– hide block

because right now it definitely reads like it's been written by someone who's seen divers jumping off boats into tropical seas on the TV, but doesn't know much else besides that. Needs a bit of Expert Witness adjustment, and although I wouldn't want to put myself forward as such because I'm an unreliable narrator and haven't been actively involved in the sport for more than ten years now, I may as well get the ball rolling because it looks like I'm the closest match at this point in time.

It's super long, but I've collapsed it as much as I reasonably can without losing the divisions (I've forgotten how to put titles on the clickables…).
+ show block
– hide block

Though I will see first whether the entire thing can be wrapped in a single collapsible with sub-collapsibles within it… edit: nope, didn't work. But if there's some special method to do that, I'm all ears. I can't seem to find a way to force line breaks after each block, either, so they're going to have to stay all jammed up against each other for now (…in fact, the line break system seems to fail entirely towards the end, which is why "6" and "8" appear mid-line … ok, weird, putting "<p>" tags in there seem to fix it, even though the tags still display as plain text instead of being incorporated into the HTML. WTF?)

1. As gung-ho as the Foundation may routinely be, especially with D-class, and as much of an adrenaline junkie (and remarkably hard to either flap or kill) 11424 is, you do NOT just take a random schlub, outfit them with SCUBA gear, and throw them in the water. That's a good way to end up with a dead or at least heavily incapacitated schlub.
+ show block
– hide block

Diving is a dangerous business, and the mere act of descending to 10+ metres depth, breathing pressurised air, and surfacing too quickly (as 11424 does) can be enough to pose a serious and immediate risk to life thanks to a little thing known as The Bends, aka decompression sickness. If the Foundation's oceangoing divisions don't know or respect this they don't really have any business putting warm bodies in the water.

Now, if the reason 11424 was selected was that he already has considerable open-water, free roaming, solo diving experience (usually you go down at least in pairs, to operate a buddy-safety system), rather than merely "always having wanted to go diving", then that would seem far more realistic. Otherwise he's potentially an anomaly to himself given his instant Mary-Sue grade expertise in several extremely technical and dangerous disciplines and resistance to being Killed To Death in ways that can only be explained through narrative impetus or other pataphysical weirdness.

2. Arctic waters, and a wetsuit? Dude's going to be dead of exposure within ten minutes.
+ show block
– hide block

There's not really any such thing as an "insulated" wetsuit (the water it traps close to the skin is meant to act as insulation), unless you mean something that has electrically-insulated heating elements in it, and arctic/antarctic water is COLD. Water is also not a particularly good insulator, in fact quite the opposite, which is why it's used as a heat transfer medium in all kinds of machinery, including steam engines and electricity generator turbines. It's merely used in wetsuits, worn in cool-to-lukewarm waters (English/Welsh coast or equivalent in spring/autumn is about your limit, and even then it can leave you shivering if you're don't keep active whilst in the water), because it's readily available, better than nothing, and means you don't need to ensure good sealing of the suit.

What you want is a drysuit.
+ show block
– hide block

Probably not even the snug neoprene type (ie wetsuit-like, but with thicker foam, integrated boots, and tight sealing cuffs at the wrists and neck) that I used for braving the already quite chilly waters around the UK (and, just once, a flooded inland quarry in February, which was damn near arctic conditions and something I have no desire to ever repeat) with some thin clothing underneath, but a baggy "membrane" type (typically a one-piece, 1~2mm thick PVC affair with integrated boots and gloves, more resembling the classical brass-helmet deep sea diver outfit) that lets you wear multiple layers of thermal underwear, socks and undergloves and puts an even greater volume of air (a much better insulator) between your skin and the water.

3. How the heck is he talking into the radio, with a regular SCUBA set? You have a pacifier-like regulator valve stuck in your mouth most of the time.
+ show block
– hide block

Jam a balled-up sock in your mouth and try to talk, and for extra credit do it with your head under water in the bath. Good luck making any kind of intelligible sounds beyond grunt-like humming noises or an "MMMMPFFFF!" of surprise. There's a reason divers often carry a writing slate or magnadoodle-type pad (which was probably the inspiration for the toy), in situations where interpersonal communication beyond the level of universally-agreed hand signals may be needed.

There's also the problem of hearing replies because your ears are either sealed-over by a foam rubber hood, or even get full of water (the hood is the one part of the suit which routinely ends up letting in water). Plus, where's the mic and speaker/earpiece located? Typical mics and speakers/earphones built to work in open air don't work very well when submerged.

However, if you have a membrane drysuit on anyway, it would make sense to pair it with a full-face polycarbonate face mask or even a full helmet.
+ show block
– hide block

That will insulate the head a lot better than a typical diving hood or balaclava (you really don't want to get brainfreeze from your head being insufficiently insulated, and even just exposing my lips and the skin around them in the quarry was rapidly painful, and then problematic for maintaining a seal around the reg once they went numb), as well as giving you the freedom to wear a thick thermal skiing balaclava or similar, and more relevantly allow you to talk freely much like any other full-face non-diving helmet.

Essentially, arctic diving is not too dissimilar from going into space. You need a full environment suit to survive for any length of time, and to perform remote voice communications.

4. There's no such thing as a "two hour" air supply, unless you know exactly what depth profile (that is, depth graphed against time) you'll follow thoughout the course of the dive. Tanks instead hold a certain mass of air
+ show block
– hide block

(essentially, the equivalent of a certain large volume, heavily compressed to fit in the tank; a common single tank may hold 12 to 15 litres, at around 300 bar (atmospheres), thus roughly equal to 3600~4500 litres at atmospheric pressure, though only about 200~250 bars of that pressure is considered usable in order to leave a safety margin, and if you let it drop below 10 bar the regulators start to malfunction - the air in the tank has to have a pressure at least 1 or 2 atm above that of the water outside, and every 10m you descend adds an extra atmosphere of water pressure),

and the amount you use per minute depends both on your activity level, and the depth to which you've descended. At 10m you use twice the air you would at the surface, 3x more at 20m, etc.
<p>
On top of which, you use some of it to both provide the insulating air inside the suit and control your buoyancy through inflating it and/or a float vest
+ show block
– hide block

(again, the deeper you go, the more compensating air you need, especially if you're naturally buoyant enough at the surface to need waistband, ankle or even wrist weights in order to descend at all in the first place). Which is something you definitely can't do with a wetsuit.

For an exploration like this you'd just outfit your diver with as much tank capacity as they could reasonably carry, probably a 24L twin-set plus a 3~7L emergency backup "pony", give them strict orders to turn back when half of it's used up (to account for getting lost, the need for decompression stops, etc) and just hope for the best.

It probably wouldn't be the worst idea ever to provide them with a supply pipe from a shipboard compressor for the initial part of the dive, too (you may recall that those brass-helmet dive outfits don't actually have air tanks - technically, they're not SCUBA as they're not self contained, but have a rescue tether and an air pipe trailing up to the boat at the surface), which will extend the dive time, particularly as the tanks themselves will be filled with Trimix (special gas mixture where some of the oxygen and a lot of the nitrogen is replaced with helium, which reduces the risk of decompression sickness, nitrogen narcosis, and oxygen toxicity at extreme pressures) if there's a risk or expectation that the diver may be descending below about 40 metres. It's not quite as easy to breathe at the lower pressures near the surface, and you don't want to go using it up if you can avoid it, because even with a large capacity twinset your breathing time at extreme depths is very limited, usually less than 30 minutes.

In fact, it would probably be best to go extremely technical, and get an experienced Dive Master (an actual title) to carry out the exploration with use of a Rebreather set,
+ show block
– hide block

which is able to recycle exhaled air in a semi-closed cycle, retaining the 15 or so percent oxygen that would otherwise be lost into the water as bubbles, chemically scrubbing out the carbon dioxide and adding in a much smaller volume of air from the tank(s) versus what would normally be needed. If you need someone to be down for hours at a time (which also implies a very extended decompression sequence, unless they never descend below about 5 metres, which is not much deeper than the bottom of a high-dive pool), can't be certain of the depths they'll reach, and can't provide a pumped air supply for the entire time, there aren't really any practical alternatives.

Unless of course we're making use of some convenient bit of anom-tech that allows extended periods of underwater breathing, like an artificial gill (something that mundane science is continually trying and failing to develop) or a portal back to (a pressurised space at) the surface…

5. You're sending him into a completely unknown underwater space, with no idea of the conditions that will be met during the exploration, but haven't outfit him with a recall tether? Or some kind of James Bond water-scooter gadget? I know D-class are disposable (if so, why not send a pair?), but you want the exploration to succeed don't you?
+ show block
– hide block

The currents even in mundane seas can be extremely dangerous for experienced divers and deadly for the inexperienced, newbies are generally taken out in waters known to be mostly still and underwater surfing / "drift" diving on riptides and other rapid undercurrents are something mainly indulged in by more experienced, thrill seeking divers - and never solo, unless truly suicidal.

And even then, the trip is specially planned to take advantage of said current, with a safe extraction point at the far end (usually, you have a shore crew who drive a boat or a suitable number of land vehicle(s) from the start point to the end, and you tag out with them for a second session where, after returning to the start, they dive and you drive), because the maximum swimming speed of even a flipper-equipped diver isn't more than 2 to 3 knots (and waterscooters rarely break double figures), so there's no way you're going to emulate a salmon and swim back upstream, even if you have exceptional stamina. And then we have the problems of getting turned around and disoriented, or ending up in turbulent, murky waters where you can't see beyond your own outstretched hand (or worse) and start to lose track of which way is up…

Sending a guy into uncharted waters without a tether, or at least a rope he can hang on to and haul himself back hand-over-hand (as used by cave divers so they can find their way back out in zero-visibility conditions, and as one of many additional duties performed by the anchor rope for the Surface Marker Buoy deployed by more mainstream open water divers - as a return point identifiable from a distance in good visibility and low current conditions, as something to hang onto and hold absolute position whilst performing decompression stops, a way of communicating with the surface crew by tugging on it morse-code style, and in extremis something to tie a buddy onto and extract them to the surface if they're incapacitated and between you there's not enough air to float them up there)…

…it's essentially a death sentence because there's no guarantee he'll have any chance of returning to the insertion point - or even being able to orient himself in turbulent waters.
<p>
6. Presumably the radios are using some kind of anom-tech transceivers, as radio doesn't work very well underwater.
+ show block
– hide block

Water may not be a very good insulator of heat, but it's quite effective against RF, ionising and particle radiation (including, beyond a few dozen metres, sunlight); it's why submarines have to surface to communicate over anything other than morse code through 100m+ towed-array VLF aerials (and have to ascend to only a few dozen metres depth to use those). What carries well in water is low frequency sound, hence the use of sonar etc. When IRL divers need to communicate with the surface, they tend to use wired intercoms with a certain length of hardline depending on application (also what's used for mini subs or USVs transmitting live video - there's no way you're getting enough bandwidth for that over radio through a water barrier), or just the aforementioned rope safety line or buoy tether which can transmit a limited number of tug-coded messages back and forth (and is much cheaper and usable with neoprene suits, mouthpiece regulators and simple eye+nose masks).

7. All the stuff the foundation has available, and they can't even equip 11424 with a knife that can cut through otherwise unremarkable fishing-net rope? That's one of the primary uses for a diving knife,
+ show block
– hide block

mundane examples of which tend to be sturdy, very sharp, and equipped with pretty vicious serrations (so you always carry it in a substantial holster to avoid accidents, and are taught to respect it). If you need some reason why his knife can't free him, simply being submerged in and having his arms pinned by a couple tonnes of fish tightly bound in a gathered-up net should be sufficient…

though it wouldn't be too much of an immersion break to outfit him with a high powered but limited-runtime wrist mounted cutting laser or something.<p>
8. I'd also have suggested he take a compass. Hopefully he at least had a depth meter and tank pressure readout (a pair of tools that are often found incorporated into a single block-like set of gauges, along with a thermometer and floating-ball compass). Either way, with all the above, it's probably unreasonable to expect he would be equipped with a dive computer
+ show block
– hide block

(a very useful thing that automatically calculates your nitrogen saturation and tells you the necessary decompression stops… and that I was able to afford a simple time-and-barometer based example of even as a university student ~15 years ago, so it'd be pocket change for the late-2010s Foundation to get a top-of-the-line model that connects a sensors in line with the high-pressure air hoses, detects heart and respiration rate, etc)

Of course, all this would negatively impact his out-of-the-water mobility, but it seems his SCUBA suit (and flippers, tanks?) went missing at some random point anyway. Hopefully the extraction team got him out of the freezing saltwater and into a well-heated decompression chamber tout suite after he bobbed to the surface…

Also, final petty point: he's equipped with "a camera". A stills camera? Video camera? Live video camera feed? ;-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creative and very original log. Maybe not much substance beyond that, but I still enjoy a well-written story, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) what the fuck
2) that's delightful, take my upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Traumatizing but fun overall"

Basically sums up my experience with this site, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never thought I would say this, but I think I have a favorite D class now. I can see why other people could get annoyed with the exploration, but I personally found D-11424's reactions to everything pretty damn funny so I don't mind. Shame he's probably doing to die if brought up in anything else on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They possess blonde hair styled in a mullet, and black leather jackets with various decorations.

Okay that sentence got better with each part.
Jesus, this whole log is a trip. It starts out with your typical exploration log, you expect the D-class to get killed by some unknown entity shortly after entering the portal, then WALRUSES, and hoo boy. It's a side of D-class we never see. It's natural and self-inserty in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, this was fun. At first I was annoyed that this was going to be another wack fest, but the more wacky it got… I sorta loved it? It's the creative, well thought out wacky with some teeny tiny amount of logic put into it.

I mean, this is just a huge world full of different things trading with each-other. The ending was actually kinda creepy too even tho he survived.

Maybe it's just me wanting to like this… I donno, at least it was fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. D-11424 best d-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. The commentary from D-11424 is the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Feels too wacky for a proper SCP. Things especially got too random after the D class went into the portal and it just completely lost me.

You don't need to be scary, but at least be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I love D-11424. Is he related to Deadpool?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Luckily for you, D-11424 has a tag, so you can find all the articles they appear in… here:

http://www.scp-wiki.net/system:page-tags/tag/d-11424#pages

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this sould be a -J. Entertaining nonetheless. I hope 11424 goes on more adventures. The juxtaposition between his reactions and the situations he finds himself in is humorous, as well as relatable in the sense that anyone would want the ability to keep their cool like that.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for Johnny Bravo walruses

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yesssss…. +1

Oddly smooth, given the many unexpected turns.
But all and all a lovely story.
That said, this portal place might be nice for some tales perhaps?

item.title: Re: The Meaty of 11424
item.content:encoded: HUGE thanks to the wonderful SunnyClockwork for creating this adorable D-11424 art: https://hisclockworkservants.tumblr.com/post/173095962305/d-11424-the-original-d-boi

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one was fun. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In which a D-Class has cartoonish and morbid adventures in Wackyland. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. This was a metric ton of what the ever loving fuck, followed by a good dash of …what?

…Just how I love my scips. +1 for this beautiful.. I dunno. It's just good dammit. Take my vote already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "traumatizing but fun overall" secured my upvote. Nice to see D-class that can roll with the punches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Authors* :P Thank you for enjoying the trip of reading like we did the trip of writing~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, it's somewhat refreshing to see someone, especially a D-class, adapt and thrive in an anomalous situation without dying by the end. But in this case, it goes on for too long, the situation seems pointless on the surface, and he's just so smug about it. So the base concept here could work, it just needs to be refocused and trimmed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the trip this article encompassed me on. Great work, author. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea, that might be the default for some, but it's far from the rule. There are examples of D-Class that are volunteers or demoted personnel, or sourced from elsewhere.

Even if you suscribe to the death row prisoner origin, there's still the fact that individuals won't react the same to any given situation, so having one that's able to keep a level head isn't completely far-fetched.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-class are death row inmates conscripted into slave labor by the foundation

That's not really standard anymore. And I figure, not every test/exploration/incident kills mountains of D-class. Surely the Foundation would prefer to send someone somewhat experienced, who won't freak out at the first sign of an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love almost everything about this. Definite +1.

But the D-class's reactions threw me off. I could maybe imagine a gung-ho and slightly crazy adventurous MTF member being this cavalier and enthusiastic about a dangerous and surreal exploration mission. But it's hard to imagine a D-class being so. Especially if we're going with the standard, "D-class are death row inmates conscripted into slave labor by the foundation" backstory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nthing the above. I'm usually a huge sucker for explorations, but there really doesn't seem to be much to this aside from the protag stumbling through a series of inane events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D-11424's wild adventure in Walrus land!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man! This reads like a good fantasy novel and I thoroughly enjoyed it. My only problem is that this is from the perspective of the d-class and not HQ but is still through the eyes of HQ (if that makes sense).

Anyways, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The goal when writing D-11424's character was to break the usual trend of D-class being scared shitless all the time. (In my headcanon, most D-class experience quite a few anomalies before dying or being rendered too anomalous for further SCP tests.)

We may have swung a bit too far in the other direction, but we love D-11424 too much to rewrite the entire thing.

The other thing I'll say is that everything in there is there for a reason; there is a point to all of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this, though I got irritated earlier than that. I think I'd be more on board with this one if there were more of a point than just a sequence of weird, loosely-connected things. The article's got this bizarre inter-dimensional trading post and these mysterious portals (how did one get into the Arctic ocean?), which I think would make a good core for an article but as it is I kept getting distracted and annoyed by 11424's relentless unflappability. I respect the weirdness, but not enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with this, this feels like one of those newer action movies that tries to compress what would have been a long serial when I was a kid into one movie by removing anything that doesn't have explosions in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
guy encounters even more inexplicavle thing than the last inexplicable thing he encountered without ever losing his chill

yes

Maybe not inexplicable things, because I like inexplicable things, but the protag's writing gets remarkably grating after a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be honest, this was interesting, but I got irritated by the exploration log about two-thirds of the way through. It just became this neverending cascade of "guy encounters even more inexplicable thing than the last inexplicable thing he encountered without ever losing his chill". It needs a trimming, if you ask me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck did I just read?
Well, here's your +1 while I go insane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the fuck? +1

item.title: The Meaty of 11424
item.content:encoded:
Wery already pretty much ‘splained how this came into existence, so I don’t have too much to say, other than I had a ton of fun writing this (and pumping out a ton of collabs recently in general). This was written more for the joy of writing it than the idea of having a phenomenal article. We had a blast writing what is essentially an entire exploration log of D-11424’s quips, and we hope that you have just about the same amount of fun reading it.

Also, hurray for the birth of the Lugwardians!

Also, here's some intensely not good art that I did.
Told ya so!

item.title: Pleased to Meat You
item.content:encoded:
This is a historic moment. To my knowledge, this is the first direct Xmas gifter-giftee collab! More personally, it's my first exploration and my longest work ever (for now!)

Our story begins on a cold December morning. DarkStuff had been selected as my gift recipient for the Christmas Exchange. I was moved by the selflessness of his request and wrote him a little something. He was very happy to receive it but did note many areas that could be improved. So we agreed to a collab, that my idea and his skills could be combined to make this work what it was always destined to be.

Fast forward a few months. After both tying up loose ends, we sat down and hammered out this draft together. I had a blast working with Stuff for this, and I hope you have as much fun reading as we did writing.

Big thanks to 9Volt does not match any existing user name, Captain Kirby, and CadaverCommander for crit.

Do enjoy your visit.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Noaa-walrus29.jpg public domain
